coredata.dc:description:
In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were loaded onto CoFe2O4 spinel oxides through a hydrothermal method to synthesize magnetic nanocomposites (CFOC0.07). The strong Fe-Co interactions in CFOC0.07 effectively suppressed Co ion leaching, while the introduction of CQDs exposed additional reactive sites. The conjugated π structure in the CQDs molecule can effectively regulate the electron transfer efficiency of CFOC0.07 and accelerate the electron transfer in the CFOC0.07/PMS system. The main active sites Co(III) and Fe(II) on CFOC0.07 synergize with the
HSO
5
∙ -
/
SO
5
∙ -
and PMS systems to promote the cycling of Co(II)/Co(III), which further enhances the catalytic ability. Experimental results demonstrated that the CFOC0.07/PMS system could remove up to 95.6% of Norfloxacin (NOR) under optimal conditions, involving both free radicals (
SO
4
∙ -
, •OH, and
O
2
∙ -
) and non-free radical (1O2) as reactive substances. Additionally, cycling experiments and a dynamic continuous flow reactor showed that CFOC0.07 exhibited high magnetic properties, durable stability and good recycling performance. Toxicity analysis demonstrated that CFOC0.07 significantly reduced the biotoxicity of NOR. This study introduces a new mechanism for efficient degradation of organic pollutants and offers innovative insights into water pollution control.

head.ce:title: Degradation of norfloxacin by highly magnetic CoFe2O4 loaded 0D-CQDS activated PMS: Mechanistic analysis and environmental impacts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northeast Electric Power University, Jilin City 132012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northeast Electric Power University, Jilin City 132012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were loaded onto CoFe2O4 spinel oxides through a hydrothermal method to synthesize magnetic nanocomposites (CFOC0.07). The strong Fe-Co interactions in CFOC0.07 effectively suppressed Co ion leaching, while the introduction of CQDs exposed additional reactive sites. The conjugated π structure in the CQDs molecule can effectively regulate the electron transfer efficiency of CFOC0.07 and accelerate the electron transfer in the CFOC0.07/PMS system. The main active sites Co(III) and Fe(II) on CFOC0.07 synergize with the
HSO
5
∙ -
/
SO
5
∙ -
and PMS systems to promote the cycling of Co(II)/Co(III), which further enhances the catalytic ability. Experimental results demonstrated that the CFOC0.07/PMS system could remove up to 95.6% of Norfloxacin (NOR) under optimal conditions, involving both free radicals (
SO
4
∙ -
, •OH, and
O
2
∙ -
) and non-free radical (1O2) as reactive substances. Additionally, cycling experiments and a dynamic continuous flow reactor showed that CFOC0.07 exhibited high magnetic properties, durable stability and good recycling performance. Toxicity analysis demonstrated that CFOC0.07 significantly reduced the biotoxicity of NOR. This study introduces a new mechanism for efficient degradation of organic pollutants and offers innovative insights into water pollution control.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater resources are vital to human survival and development, with freshwater constituting just 2.5 % of global water reserves. The rate of global freshwater consumption has increased by approximately 1.0 % per year over the past few years. Concurrently, as the world population surges, the urgency for freshwater resources intensifies. Consequently, wastewater purification and reuse have emerged as crucial components of the freshwater cycle[1]. Antibiotics are a class of drugs that are vital for treating human and animal diseases, as well as promoting animal growth. However, following ingestion, these antibiotics are metabolized by organisms and subsequently discharged into domestic wastewater[2,3]. Domestic wastewater containing a variety of antibiotics traverses the water cycle, resulting in the detectable presence of antibiotics (typically at concentrations below 10 µg/L) in numerous aquatic systems, including groundwater, lakes, seawater, and even drinking water. Furthermore, the accumulation of antibiotics in water bodies exacerbates bacterial resistance, jeopardizing beneficial microbial communities and posing health hazards to both humans and animals[4]. As an example, Norfloxacin (NOR), a second-generation synthetic fluoroquinolone antibiotic, mainly acts on DNA cleavage enzymes in bacteria to inhibit DNA synthesis and eliminate bacteria[5]. NOR has been utilized in the treatment of diseases such as enteritis and dysentery. However, its utilization in living organisms is only 30.0–70.0 %, which makes it more difficult to degrade than other organic pollutants, including oxytetracycline, tetracycline hydrochloride, and various dyes[6]. Norfloxacin (NOR) poses significant environmental risks as it is carcinogenic, non-biodegradable, and exhibits high toxicity when its concentration in water reaches certain levels. In order to address the issue of NOR removal from wastewater, a number of conventional approaches have been employed, including physical adsorption[7], chemical precipitation[8,9] and biodegradation[10]. However, these methods fall short in achieving complete degradation of NOR, often resulting in low removal efficiency and the generation of secondary pollutants. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a wastewater treatment technology that removes NOR from the environment and reduces the need to generate secondary pollution.

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), which use reactive oxygen species (ROS) to remove organic pollutants from wastewater, are widely used to treat a variety of antibiotic wastewater[11,12]. In particular, persulfate advanced oxidation technology has received widespread attention due to its remarkable stability and cost-effectiveness[9,13]. Persulfates are classified as peroxymonosulfates (PMS,
HSO
5
-
) and peroxydisulfates (PDS,
S 2
O
8
2-
). Notably, the molecular architecture of PMS (H-O-O-SO3) exhibits greater asymmetry compared to PDS, leading to more localized electron distribution in PMS. This characteristic facilitates its facile reduction to radicals, such as •OH and
SO
4
∙ -
, upon accepting electrons. Of these radicals, sulfate radical (
SO
4
∙ -
) has higher oxidation potential (2.7 V ∼ 3.1 V) and longer half-life (30 μs ∼ 40 μs)[14,15]. Furthermore, PMS can form anionic radicals (
SO
5
∙ -
) by oxidation, which subsequently combine with water molecules to yield 1O2 [16]. Consequently, PMS has emerged as a focal point in research endeavors. The activation strategies for persulfates encompass energy-driven methods (such as light, heat, electricity)[17–20], transition metal catalysis[21], and spinel ferrite activation[22]. Each approach carries its unique merits and limitations, however, all of them require energy or catalyst activation for their activation methods.

In recent years, bimetallic iron-based oxides (MFe2O4) have attracted attention for their efficient degradation of various organic compounds through the efficient activation of PMS by electron transfer, where M represents elements such as Co, Cu and Mn. Notably, spinel ferrite CoFe2O4 nanoparticles are magnetic and can be easily recovered from wastewater. Among them, the strong Fe-Co interaction inhibits the leaching of Co ions and the efficient activation of PMS[23]. However, the d-orbital of elemental Co in CoFe2O4 is occupied by five electrons, leading to strong electron repulsion between the Co center and the surrounding oxygen ligands, limiting the catalytic performance of CoFe2O4 [24]. Moreover, these particles tend to aggregate, exacerbating the issue of secondary pollution during reactions. To mitigate these drawbacks, anchoring the nanoparticles onto appropriate stabilizing materials presents a practical and straightforward solution. Researchers have explored various methods to solve this problem, such as combining different carbons, biochar, biomass, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), and natural materials with CoFe2O4 nanoparticles[25]. For instance, Pan et al.[26] introduced carbon nitride to modify CoFe2O4 nanoparticles, enhancing the activation of PMS. This modification significantly improved the charge carrier separation efficiency and electron transfer rate, resulting in an 86.0 % removal rate of tetracycline (TC) within 10 min. Similarly, Li et al.[27] loaded CoFe2O4 nanoparticles onto activated carbon using the urea combustion method. This approach effectively reduced the dissolution of cobalt ions and achieved a degradation rate of 95.1 % for Orange I (OI). Among these materials, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials, are widely used for their high biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and inexpensive sources[28–30]. Typically, CQDs consist of sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon substrates attached to oxygen-containing functional groups[31]. Additionally, the conjugated π-structure in CQDs endows them with remarkable electron storage and fast electron transport properties[32]. Consequently, loading CQDs onto CoFe2O4 to activate PMS is a feasible strategy.

In this study, CQDs were loaded onto CoFe2O4 via a hydrothermal method to synthesize magnetic nanocomposites with high saturated magnetic strength and enhanced catalytic properties (CFOC0.07). The incorporation of CQDs significantly accelerated the electron transfer rate in the CFOC0.07/PMS system, promoting the redox rates of Co(III)/Co(II) and Fe(III)/Fe(II) on the CFOC0.07 nanoparticles and thus enhancing the catalytic activity of CFOC0.07. Through quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis, we proposed a mechanism for the generation of active species within the CFOC0.07/PMS system. Our novel approach of loading carbon materials onto spinel oxides offers advantages such as ease of operation, economic feasibility, and environmental friendliness, proposing a new idea for the degradation of difficult-to-degrade antibiotic systems.

Potassium persulfate (KHSO5), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), doxycycline (DOX), levofloxacin (LVFX), oxytetracycline (OTC), and norfloxacin (NOR) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Technologies. Cobalt Chloride Hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa) and ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was sourced from Tianjin Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co. Ltd. Ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH) and hydrogen peroxide (30 % H2O2) were acquired from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Glucose (C6H12O6H2O) was bought from Shenyang Huadong Reagent Factory Co. Methanol (MeOH), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), and tert-butanol (TBA) were obtained from Liaoning Quanrui Reagent Co. P-benzoquinone (p-BQ) was procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, and L-histidine (L-His) was purchased from Aldrich Chemical Reagent Co. All experimental reagents used were analytical grade and did not require further purification.
Ultrafine CoFe2O4 spinel nanoparticles were prepared by a hydrothermal method, details of which are in Supplementary Text S1. Preparation information for CQDs is in Supplementary Text S2.

The CFOC0.07 composite catalyst was prepared using a hydrothermal method. First, 1.33 mM of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.67 mM of CoCl2·6H2O were dissolved in 20 mL of ethylene glycol. Then, 10.97 mM of anhydrous sodium acetate was added to the solution, which was ultrasonicated for 20 min until complete dissolution. Under magnetic stirring, 1 mol/L NaOH was added dropwise to adjust the pH of the solution to 12. A certain amount of CQDs was then mixed into the reactor and maintained at 200℃ for 6 h. The collected products were washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, and dried at 60℃. The resulting composite material is denoted as CFOCx, where “ x” represent the dosages of CQDs.
Information on degradation experimental methods and characterization techniques are in Supplementary Texts S3 and S4, respectively.

We investigated the effects of NOR self-decomposition, PMS, CoFe2O4, CFOC0.07, CoFe2O4/PMS, and CFOC0.07/PMS on the removal of NOR at ambient temperature. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the NOR solution exhibited almost no degradation within 20 min, indicating that NOR does not undergo decomposition in aqueous solution in the absence of additional factors. Thus, interference from NOR's self-degradation in other systems can be excluded. Additionally, the removal rate of NOR in the systems with PMS, CoFe2O4, and CFOC0.07 were 1.8 %, 4.1 %, and 8.9 %, respectively. These results suggest that oxidation by PMS alone has almost no effect on NOR degradation, and the adsorption performances of CoFe2O4 and CFOC0.07 are limited, necessitating the addition of an oxidizing agent to activate the catalyst activity. In the CoFe2O4/PMS system, the removal of NOR reached 65.9 % within 20 min, indicating that CoFe2O4 exhibits excellent catalytic performance for PMS[33]. However, there is still much room for improvement in this system. Specifically, the NOR removal efficiency increased to 79.7 % within 20 min after adding CQDs to the CoFe2O4/PMS system. This enhancement can be attributed to the distinctive electronic characteristics of CQDs, which accelerate electron transfer and boost the catalytic activity of the CFOC0.07/PMS system[34]. In Fig. 1(b), the first-order kinetic rate constant of the CFOC0.07/PMS system is 0.0822 min−1, indicating that CFOC0.07 has the strongest ability to activate PMS.

The study investigated the NOR degradation performance by CFOCx activated PMS. In Fig. 1(c), the removal of NOR by CoFe2O4 was 65.9 %. All composites CFOCx exhibited superior activation properties. This is because metal (Fe, Co) atoms modifying carbon materials can optimize the sp2 local electronic structure of carbon, thus promoting carbon catalysis. Furthermore, CQDs are an excellent catalyst for carbon nanomaterials, which can better activate PMS to generate free radicals and oxidized holes, thereby improving the catalytic perform This improvement could be attributed to the structural distortion of the material resulting from CQDs doping, which creates additional active sites for CFOC0.07 [34]. However, when the quantity of CQDs was augmented to 0.09 g, the elimination of NOR diminished to 76.6 %. The corresponding kobs value decreased from 0.0814 min−1 to 0.0753 min−1. The possible reason for this is that excess CQDs can affect the reaction with PMS by acting as carrier recombination centers or by occupying active sites on the surface of CoFe2O4 crystals, thus reducing the catalytic efficiency[35]. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests can reveal the charge transfer behavior at the catalyst interface. In Fig. S1, CFOC0.09 shows a larger semicircle, indicating that loading more CQDs increases the charge transfer resistance and thus reduces the electron transfer rate[36,37]. Therefore, loading appropriate amounts of CQDs can increase the charge separation rate and thus the degradation efficiency. Fig. 1(d) shows the linear fitting curve of CFOCx composites for NOR degradation. The kobs values for NOR removal under different systems are in the following order: CFOC0.07(0.0814 min−1) > CFOC0.09(0.0753 min−1) > CFOC0.05(0.0637 min−1) > CFOC0.03(0.0616 min−1) > CFOC0.01(0.0598 min−1) > CoFe2O4(0.0562 min−1). The results indicate that CFOC0.07 is an excellent catalyst with high catalytic efficiency and optimal physicochemical properties.ance of the composites[38]. The removal of NOR increased from 68.4 % for CFOC0.01 to 79.7 % for CFOC0.07 in 20 min when the addition of CQDs in CFOCx was increased from 0.01 g to 0.07 g.

The morphology of pure CoFe2O4 and composite CFOC0.07 was analyzed by Scanning electron microscope (SEM). In Fig. 2 (a), the CoFe2O4 catalyst has a fine and uniform particle morphology. Fig. 2(b) shows the Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CQDs, where the shape of CQDs nanoparticles is clearly visible and has an ultrafine size. The particle size distribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(c), which ranges from 2.0 to 22.0 nm. Compared with the CoFe2O4 catalysts alone, the surface of CFOC0.07 in Fig. 2(d) appears rough with tiny-sized clumps, which will bring these highly active CoFe2O4 sites together to provide active interfaces and diffusion channels for reactants. The Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) elemental analysis is illustrated in Fig. 2(e-i). The presence of the cobalt (27.9 %), iron (42.6 %), oxygen (17.9 %), and carbon (11.6 %) in CFOC0.07 provides further evidence that the synthesis of CFOC0.07 was successful. Fig. 2(j) illustrates the preparation process of CFOC0.07 synthesized via the hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elemental valence and crystalline phase structure analysis of CFOC0.07 catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: A study of the elemental composition and chemical valence states on the surface of CFOC0.07 was conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3 (a) shows the fully measured XPS spectrum of CFOC0.07, indicating the presence of characteristic peaks of C 1 s, Co 2p, Fe 2p and O 1 s in the composite catalyst. Since Fe and Co ions are the main active sites on CFOC0.07, we analyzed the Fe 2p and Co 2p spectra before and after CFOC0.07 use. In the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3(b)), the chemical valence states of Co in CFOC0.07 are all present as Co(II) and Co(III). The peaks at 782.9 eV, 798.9 eV, 780.5 eV and 795.8 eV of the binding energy belong to Co(II) 2p3/2, Co(II) 2p1/2, Co(III) 2p3/2 and Co(III) 2p1/2, respectively[39,40]. The two satellite peaks at 786.2 eV and 802.7 eV represent the presence of Co(II)[41]. After the CFOC0.07 reaction, the Co(III) content increased from 52.7 % to 53.4 % and the Co(II) content decreased from 47.3 % to 46.6 %. Fig. 3(c) presents the Fe 2p spectra of the CFOC0.07 catalyst. The peaks at 710.3 eV, 723.6 eV, 712.7 eV, and 726.5 eV are assigned to the Fe(II) 2p3/2, Fe(II) 2p1/2, Fe(III) 2p3/2, and Fe(III) 2p1/2, respectively. This evidence suggests the presence of oxides of Fe(II)/(III). This indicates the presence of two valence states of iron in CFOC0.07. Additionally, the two peaks at 718.9 eV and 733.1 eV are satellite peaks of Fe(III)[42,43]. And after the reaction, the content of Fe(II) increased from 51.9 % to 53.9 %, while the content of Fe(III) decreased from 48.1 % to 46.1 %. At the same time, the Co(II)/Co(III) ratio of this system decreased from 0.89 to 0.58 and the Fe(II)/(III) ratio increased from 1.07 to 1.17 after the reaction.. This phenomenon may be attributed to the change in the valence state of the metal during the activation of CFOC0.07 by PMS, suggesting an electron transfer between Fe and Co ions. The existence of synergistic interaction between Fe and Co in CFOC0.07 was confirmed. Fig. S2 shows the C 1 s spectra, where three distinct peaks can be observed at 284.5 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C-O-C, and C=O bonds, respectively[27,40]. In Fig.S3, the three peaks in the O 1 s spectrum of CFOC0.07 belong to lattice oxygen (OL), oxygen vacancies (OVS), and surface adsorbed oxygen (OA), respectively[43]. The X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) spectra of CoFe2O4 and CFOC0.07 samples are depicted in Fig. 3(d). For pure CoFe2O4, seven characteristic peaks are observed at 2θ = 18.4°, 30.5°, 35.9°, 44.1°, 53.9°, 57.3°, and 62.9°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) facets of spinel CoFe2O4, respectively[44,45]. These peaks align perfectly with the standard pattern of CoFe2O4 (PDF# 22–1086), confirming the successful synthesis of CoFe2O4. It is noteworthy that the absence of peaks associated with CQDs in CFOC0.07 may be attributed to their low content in the composite and poor crystallinity[46]. Further insights into the chemical structure and vibrational functional groups of the materials were gained through Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) analysis of individual CoFe2O4, CQDs, and CFOC0.07 nanocomposite catalysts over the range of 4000–400 cm−1, with the results illustrated in Fig. 3(e). The presence of two particular absorption peaks at 400 cm−1 and 552 cm−1 for CoFe2O4 is related to the stretching vibrations of the metal oxides in the Co(II)–O2- and Fe(III)–O2- groups of CoFe2O4 [44]. The broad peak observed at 3451 cm−1 is indicative of an O–H stretching vibration. The peaks at 2971 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 are ascribed to C–H stretching for sp2 and sp3, respectively. The peaks at 1714 cm−1, 1491 cm−1, and 1064 cm−1 are attributed to C=O, C=C, and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively[28,30,31]. In the CFOC0.07 composite catalyst, all characteristic bonds from both CoFe2O4 and CQDs, including Fe-O, Co-O, O–H, and C–H bonds, are clearly present. This not only confirms the coexistence of both components in the composite but also suggests that the integration of CQDs helps maintain the chemical stability of the CoFe2O4 structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different catalytic reaction conditions on NOR degradation efficiency
Effect of catalyst and PMS dosage
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of varying catalyst dosages and PMS concentrations on the catalytic activity of the composite CFOC0.07 were systematically investigated to establish the optimal operating conditions. In Fig. 4 (a), the NOR removal efficiency demonstrated a direct proportionality with the increase in CFOC0.07 dosage. Specifically, an increase in the dosage of the catalyst from 50 mg/L to 200 mg/L resulted in a notable enhancement in NOR removal efficiency, rising from 61.5 % to 80.4 %. This was accompanied by a corresponding rise in kobs, from 0.0499 min−1 to 0.0764 min−1 within 20 min. This enhancement is attributed to the fact that higher doses of CFOC0.07 provide more active sites for PMS to generate more ROS and accelerate NOR degradation. However, the degradation efficiency of NOR by CFOC0.07 was slightly decreased by 2.6 % when the catalyst dosage was increased to 250 mg/L. This is because the limited amount of PMS would limit the reaction and thus affect the degradation efficiency of NOR. Consequently, a dosage of 200 mg/L for CFOC0.07 was determined to be sufficient for effectively activating PMS for NOR degradation.

In Fig. 4(b), the removal rate of NOR showed a positive correlation with PMS dosage. Specifically, upon increasing the PMS dosage from 0.05 mM to 0.20 mM, a notable enhancement in NOR removal was observed, rising from 77.7 % to 90.7 %. Concurrently, the kobs exhibited a gradual increase, progressing from 0.0708 min−1 to 0.1086 min−1. The data suggests that an increase in the quantity of PMS results in the formation of a greater number of reactive radicals within the reaction system. Nevertheless, when the PMS dose was augmented to 0.25 mM, the NOR removal rate diminished to 89.3 %, and the kobs dropped to 0.1007 min−1. One reason could be the limited active sites produced by CFOC0.07 at the same dosage of CFOC0.07. Another reason is that the excess of
HSO
5
-
(E0 = 1.8 V) reacted with
SO
4
∙ -
(E0 = 2.5–3.1 V) to produce low oxidizing capacity
SO
5
∙ -
and
H O
2
∙
(Eqs. (1) and (2)). These substances scavenge ROS, which reduces the removal efficiency of NOR[45,47]. In conclusion, while increasing PMS dosage can enhance the reaction rate up to a point, excessive amounts not only fail to improve the rate but also pose potential environmental hazards. Therefore, the optimal PMS dosage is determined to be 0.20 m M.

To verify the catalytic activity of CFOC0.07, different concentrations of NOR solutions were selected. Fig. 4(c) shows the removal of NOR at different concentrations (5 mg/L − 25 mg/L) by the CFOC0.07/PMS system. The experimental conditions used were 200 mg/L CFOC0.07 and 0.20 mM PMS. A negative correlation between NOR removal rate and concentration is observed. Even at a NOR concentration of 25 mg/L, the removal rate could still attain 89.1 %. As the NOR concentration decreased, the removal rate increased, reaching a value of 95.7 %. This is because when the catalyst and PMS dosage are fixed, the generated ROS are limited, and not enough free radicals are produced, which hinders the degradation of NOR at higher concentrations. Another reason is that higher concentrations of NOR produce more intermediates adsorbed on the catalyst surface, so that the contact of CFOC0.07 with ROS is inhibited. Nevertheless, the degradation efficiency and kobs remained almost unchanged when the NOR concentration was varied from 5 mg/L to 10 mg/L. To address a more practical antibiotic wastewater scenario, the NOR concentration was therefore set at 10 mg/L.

Fig. 4(d) illustrates the degradation curves of NOR at varying temperatures to assess the impact of temperature on catalytic activity. As the temperature rose from 15℃ to 55℃, the CFOC0.07/PMS system demonstrated an increase in NOR degradation efficiency from 91.7 % to 98.3 % within 20 min, accompanied by a rise in kobs from 0.1062 min−1 to 0.1429 min−1. The results indicate that high temperature promotes the production of more
SO
4
∙ -
(Eq. (3)) in the CFOC0.07/PMS system, which accelerates the degradation of NOR[28]. It is also demonstrated that the system is a heat-absorbing reaction, and the high temperature accelerates the rate of molecular diffusion, which enhances the irregular movement of molecules in the solution toward the catalyst surface[48]. However, high temperatures can lead to increased energy losses, so there are cost considerations for practical wastewater applications. The activation energy (Ea) of the system was calculated using the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (4)), where A is the prefactor, R (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1) is the ideal gas constant, T (Kelvin) is the temperature, and Ea (kJ·mol−1) is the activation energy. In Fig. S4, the correlation between 1/T and kinetic constants (lnk) demonstrates a strong linear relationship with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.96. Based on this correlation, the activation energy for CFOC0.07 degrading NOR was determined to be 5.4 kJ·mol−1, which is significantly lower than that reported for N, S, O co-doped Fe0@Fe3O4/PMS (52.7 kJ·mol−1)[49] and Ce-doped CuCoO2/PMS (66.2 kJ·mol−1)[47]. This comparison highlights that CFOC0.07 exhibits higher catalytic activity and greater ability to activate PMS compared to the previously reported catalyst materials.

In Fig. 4(e), the removal of NOR at pH = 3 was 76.4 % at 20 min. This is because H+ removes part of the •OH and

As the pH rose from 3 to 9, the order of NOR removal was pH = 5.8 (95.6 %) > pH = 9 (93.4 %) > pH = 7 (90.7 %) > pH = 5 (88.2 %) > pH = 3 (76.4 %). This indicates that the CFOC0.07/PMS system can degrade NOR effectively over a wide pH range (3–9). This effectiveness is attributed to the ability of

At pH = 11, the removal of NOR decreased significantly to 23.6 % (kobs of 0.0148 min−1), indicating that NOR degradation was not favored at a strongly alkaline. This may be due to the fact that the self-decomposition of PMS occurs more rapidly under alkaline conditions compared to acidic and neutral conditions[52]. Additionally, as the reaction progresses, generated reactive substances such as
SO
4
∙ -
react with OH– to produce •OH, which competes with OH– for H2O production, reducing the overall production of free radicals and causing a sharp decline in the NOR removal rate. Fig. 4(f) demonstrates the catalytic performance of the catalyst by comparing the CFOC0.07/PMS system with previously reported PMS systems[53–58]. The specific parameters are detailed in Table S1. This comparison highlights that the CFOC0.07/PMS system exhibits a shorter reaction time and higher degradation efficiency. Additionally, CFOC0.07 is magnetic and easily recoverable. These results indicate that the CFOC0.07/PMS system has excellent catalytic activity and has great potential for the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential for practical application of CFOC0.07
ce:section.ce:para:
In practical wastewater treatment, antibiotic-containing wastewater often contains inorganic salts with different anions that compete with free radicals, potentially hindering the catalytic activity of CFOC0.07 composites and affecting the degradation of target pollutants. Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the degradation studies of NOR by different anions in the CFOC0.07/PMS system. The degradation of NOR was minimally inhibited by
SO

To assess the broad applicability of the CFOC0.07 catalyst, five distinct antibiotic degradation experiments were conducted as shown in Fig. 5(b). The results revealed that within a 20-minute timeframe, the CFOC0.07/PMS system achieved degradation efficiencies for various antibiotics in the following order: oxytetracycline (OTC) (98.8 %) > norfloxacin (NOR) (95.6 %) > levofloxacin (LVFX) (93.1 %) > doxycycline (DOX) (84.4 %) > sulfamethoxazole (SMX) (84.2 %). These results indicate that CFOC0.07 is a catalytic material with a wide range of applications. Additionally, the effect of CFOC0.07 with different combinations of oxidizers on NOR removal was investigated in Fig. 5(c). The CFOC0.07/PMS system exhibited the highest removal of NOR compared to the CFOC0.07/PS and CFOC0.07/H2O2 systems, which could reach 95.6 % in 20 min. The results indicated that PMS was the best oxidizer for CFOC0.07. Furthermore, the application of the CFOC0.07/PMS system in real water bodies was examined. The removal effect of NOR in different water bodies is compared in Fig. 5(d). The degradation efficiency of the CFOC0.07/PMS system on NOR could reach more than 91.5 % under all water quality conditions, highlighting the practical potential of the CFOC0.07/PMS system in wastewater treatment applications. The potential of the magnetic properties and stability of CFOC0.07 for application in real wastewater was verified by recyclability experiments. After the reaction, CFOC0.07 was recovered by the magnet (Fig. S5), and the mass ratio after recovery reached more than 94.4 %. The results showed that CFOC0.07 has high magnetic properties. Fig. 5(e) shows that the removal efficiency of NOR can still reach 89.8 % after five degradation cycles. We also analyzed the leaching concentrations of Co and Fe metal ions in the CFOC0.07/PMS system and the results are shown in Fig. S6. After five cycles, the leaching concentrations of Co and Fe ions were 0.35 mg/L and 0.12 mg/L, respectively, which were lower than the Chinese water quality discharge standard (GB 25467–2010)[61]. This indicates that the CFOC0.07/PMS system has excellent stability and reusable performance. In addition, the leached Co and Fe ions had almost no effect on the degradation of NOR compared to the single PMS system (Fig. S7), suggesting that the contribution of leached metal ions to the degradation of NOR in the CFOC0.07/PMS system was negligible. Fig. 5(f) shows the XRD comparisons of CFOC0.07 before and after use, and the characteristic peaks of CFOC0.07 remain basically unchanged after use. Overall, the results demonstrate that the CFOC0.07 exhibits good catalytic properties, reusable properties, and stability, making it an excellent catalytic material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation mechanisms in the CFOC0.07/PMS system

ce:section.ce:para:
The objective of this study was to ascertain the active species produced in the CFOC0.07/PMS system. To this end, the effects of diverse radical scavengers on the degradation efficiency of NOR were examined. Methanol (MeOH), Tert-butanol (TBA), 1,4-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and L-histidine (L-His)[39,41] were added to the reaction system as radical scavengers for •OH and
SO
4
∙ -
、•OH、
O
2
∙ -
and 1O2, respectively. In Fig. 6 (a), the strongest inhibition of NOR degradation was observed with the addition of 5 mM MeOH, and the removal rate was only 22.6 %. In contrast, the removal of NOR was 85.7 % with the addition of 5 mM TBA, indicating that MeOH inhibited NOR degradation more strongly than TBA. This suggests that both MeOH and TBA mask the catalyst surface during PMS activation, reducing the generation of ROS on the CFOC0.07 catalyst surface. Therefore, the contribution of •OH to NOR removal is more limited, while
SO
4
∙ -
plays a dominant role in the system. The degradation efficiency of NOR was 77.2 % when 1 mM L-His was added, indicating that the CFOC0.07/PMS system produced less 1O2. The NOR removal rate was 42.9 % when 2 mM p-BQ was added, indicating that the inhibitory effect of
O
2
∙ -
on NOR degradation was greater than that of 1O2. We tested the consumption efficiency of PMS in the CFOC0.07/PMS system in quenching experiments to verify whether it affects the activation ability of PMS. In Fig. S8, the utilization rates of PMS with the addition of quencher were all lower than that of PMS without quencher, which may be due to the fact that the quencher can absorb PMS and affect the reaction with CFOC0.07 [62,63]. In summary, the
SO
4
∙ -
, •OH, and
O
2
∙ -
radical pathways, as well as the 1O2 non-radical pathway, were all involved in the degradation of NOR. To further demonstrate the generation of reactive species, EPR tests were conducted using DMPO and TEMP as traps for •OH,
SO
4
∙ -
,
O
2
∙ -
, and 1O2 for the detection of ROS produced in the CFOC0.07/PMS system. Details of the EPR tests are in Supplementary Text S5. Fig. 6(b) shows the characteristic peaks of 1:2:2:1 •OH and six
SO
4
∙ -
signal peaks detected when the catalyst and PMS coexisted. When 1O2 was captured using TEMP, a 1:1:1 1O2 signal peak was detected in Fig. 6(c). Fig. 6(d) shows the strong
O
2
∙ -
signaling peaks detected using DMPO, indicating a strong PMS activation capability[45]. Finally, it was demonstrated that the CFOC0.07 was able to activate PMS to produce •OH,
SO
4
∙ -
, 1O2, and
O
2
∙ -
.

The electrochemical impedance indicates the charge transfer performance of the catalyst, where the smaller the arc radius, the lower the carrier transfer resistance[64]. In Fig. 6(e), the Nyquist plot of CFOC0.07 shows the smallest arc radius, and exhibits the largest charge transfer capacity, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs promotes the electron transfer on CFOC0.07. The free corrosion potentials are shown in Fig. 6(f). The free corrosion potentials of CoFe2O4, CQDs, and CFOC0.07 are −0.02 V, −0.04 V, and −0.14 V, respectively. The aforementioned values indicate that the electron transfer rate of CFOC0.07 is accelerated subsequent to the modification of CoFe2O4 via the CQDs method. In summary, the CFOC0.07 composite exhibits the best electron transfer ability.

The composition of substances produced during NOR degradation was analyzed by two-dimensional fluorescence. In Fig. 6(g), the intensity of synchronous fluorescence increased with degradation time. Fig. 6(h) shows that the synchronized fluorescence two-dimensional spectrum has a strong autocorrelation peak near the wavelength of (350 nm,350 nm). In the asynchronous fluorescence spectra (Fig. 6(i)), negative correlation peaks were observed at (360 nm, 435 nm). By analyzing the sign of these negatively correlated peaks according to Noda's rule, the sequence order of fluorescence changes was deduced to be: 435 nm > 360 nm, indicating that humic acid analogs degrade before aromatic proteins. The 2DCOS results demonstrate alterations in the order structure of the fluorescent components generated during the degradation of NOR by CFOC0.07.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation mechanism of NOR degradation by CFOC0.07 activated PMS
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction mechanism of the CFOC0.07/PMS system was analyzed based on burst assay and EPR tests (Fig. 7 ). PMS can be activated by the active sites on CFOC0.07 through electron transfer, which is divided into radical pathways (•OH,
SO
4
∙ -
, and
O
2
∙ -
) and non-radical pathways (1O2) for NOR degradation. In the free radical pathway, when the CFOC0.07/PMS system activates PMS, the main active sites on CFOC0.07, which are mainly in the form of Co(II) and Fe(III) prior to the reaction, are converted to Co(III) and Fe(II) upon reaction with
H
SO
5
∙ -
on the catalyst surface. Then it continues to be converted to Co(II) and Fe(III) by reaction with
H
SO
5

(Eqs. (13) and (14)). The active substances in the catalyst can be reacted by the change of standard potential. Among them, the standard potential of Co(III)/Co(II) (1.8 V) is higher than that of

The standard potential of Fe(III)/Fe(II) (0.77 V) is lower than that of Co(III)/Co(II) (1.8 V), so that the Co(III) and Fe(II) can undergo a redox reaction to promote the production of Co(II)and Fe(III) production (Eqs. (15) and (16))[43].Biomass CQDs as acceptors and donors of electrons can effectively capture the carrier electrons in the CFOC0.07/PMS system, providing charge transfer to facilitate the conversion of Co(III)/Co(II) and Fe(III)/Fe(II) valence cycles[46]. Meanwhile the CQDs on CFOC0.07 and PMS in solution can absorb electrons to produce •OH and

In order to predict the active sites where the reactants tend to react with the NOR molecules, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed by combining the molecular orbital theory and the Fukui function. Among them, the main active substances in the CFOC0.07/PMS system,
SO
4
∙ -
and •OH, are electrophilic and more likely to attack the active sites on the NOR molecules that are prone to lose electrons[66]. Fig. 8 (a) shows the optimized structural model of the NOR molecule. In Fig. 8(b), the blue and green regions are negatively charged, indicating that sites in these regions are susceptible to electrophilic reactions. Fig. 8(c) illustrates that sites on O18, O19, and O20 on the NOR molecule are susceptible to electrophilic reactions, while sites on the quinolone ring and the intermediate benzene ring are susceptible to nucleophilic reactions. Nevertheless, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) cannot be used as direct predictors of the reactivity of individual groups or atoms in a molecule. Therefore, the Fukui index was employed to further predict the active sites in NOR molecules that are susceptible to reaction. Where the site values of f-, f+, and f0 in the Fukui index indicate the sensitivity to electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attack, respectively[67]. Table S2 shows the corresponding Fukui index values.

The possible degradation pathways generated by NOR were inferred in combination with Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Fig. 8(d)). The corresponding mass spectra are shown in Fig. S9, and the detailed parameters of the 18 intermediates are given in Table S3. In pathway I, C=O replaces hydrogen atoms on the piperazine ring to give P1 (m/z = 334), which undergoes oxidation to produce P2 (m/z = 350). Because N4 has a high f- value (0.030) and f0 value (0.027), it is susceptible to ring-opening reactions by
SO
4
∙ -
, •OH, and 1O2 to form P3 (m/z = 322), which continues to be oxidized to form P4 (m/z = 279)[68,69].The –CO group of P4 was further lost and the piperazine ring was broken to generate P5 (m/z = 253). F21 has high f+ (0.035) and f0 (0.033) values and is susceptible to
O
2
∙ -
and 1O2 attack for defluorination to produce P6 (m/z = 233). Continued loss of an amino group generates P7 (m/z = 218)[70,71]. Path II reflects the defluorination conversion process. C12, C14, and C15 all have high f+ (0.048, 0.027, and 0.013) and f0 (0.043, 0.013, and 0.080) values, and are susceptible to
O
2
∙ -
and 1O2 attacks. Causes NOR to undergo C-F bond breaking to generate P8 (m/z = 302)[6]. Then, hydroxyl substitution occurs to produce P9 (m/z = 318)[72]. The C-COOH bond of P9 is broken to form P10 (m/z = 274)[73]. Subsequent hydrolysis of the intermediate to P11 (m/z = 292)[74]. In pathway III, C13, C17, and O20 have higher f- values (0.064, 0.061, and 0.181) and f0 values (0.040, 0.055, and 0.123), and are more susceptible to attack by
SO
4
∙ -
, •OH, and 1O2. Opening of the quinolone ring in the NOR by –OH and R-O-oxidation generates P13 (m/z = 352)[69]. In addition, the high f+ (0.107) and f0 (0.101) values of O18 were susceptible to attack by
O
2
∙ -
and 1O2, which caused P13 to undergo decarbonylation and decarboxylation to produce P14 (m/z = 296) and further decarboxylation to produce P15 (m/z = 268). P15 was subjected to an attack by –OH, resulting in the loss of its carboxyl group and the formation of P16 (m/z = 251). This process continued with further degradation leading to the formation of P17 (m/z = 121) and P18 (m/z = 107). Eventually, as the active substance continues to invade, the NOR completely breaks down into smaller inorganic molecules such as CO2, H2O,
N
O
3
-
and F-.

The toxicity of NOR to three aquatic organisms (fish, daphnia, and green algae) was evaluated using the ECOSAR (Ecosarapplication64) software. The biotoxicity grading criteria were as follows: highly toxic < 1, 1 < toxic < 10, 10 < harmful < 100, and 100 < harmless[75] (Table S4). Fig. 9 (a-b) show that most of the intermediates were harmless. In acute toxicity, only intermediate P9 was harmful to daphnia. For chronic toxicity, intermediates P10 and P13 were harmful to all three aquatic organisms, while intermediate P9 was toxic to fish and daphnia and harmful to green algae. However, intermediate P9 is converted to other harmless intermediates as the reaction progresses. Additionally, the bioconcentration factor, mutagenicity, and developmental toxicity of NOR and its intermediates were evaluated using the TEST (Version 5.1.2, US EPA) software (Table S5). Fig. 9(c) shows that the BCFs of intermediates P10, P15, and P16 were greater than those of NOR, indicating that they are more easily enriched in organisms than NOR. Fig. 9(d) indicates that among the three degradation pathways, intermediates generated at the beginning were more mutagenic and developmentally toxic than NOR. However, as the reaction progressed, intermediates P6, P7, P12, P17, and P18 transformed into nontoxic and negative forms, aligning with predictions by ECOSAR software. Overall, most intermediates were harmless, and final products were non-toxic with no adverse effects on organisms. This demonstrates that the CFOC0.07/PMS system is an efficient and non-polluting method for removing toxic organic pollutants. In addition to theoretical predictions using ECOSAR and TEST software, seed toxicity experiments were conducted to detect biotoxicity of intermediates produced during NOR degradation. Fig. 9 (e-f) shows the inhibition rate of seed germination by NOR at different times. The germination rate under natural conditions was 100 %. In 10 mg/L undegraded NOR wastewater, germination was 0 %. Seed germination inhibition rate was 80.0 % and 45.0 % at degradation times of 2 and 5 min, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9(e), only a few seeds germinated within the first 5 min. The inhibition of seed germination by NOR decreased to 30.0 %, 10.0 % and 5.0 % when the time reached 10, 15 and 20 min, respectively. These experimental results confirmed that the biotoxicity in NOR wastewater was significantly reduced after the treatment of NOR wastewater with CFOC0.07/PMS system. It shows that CFOC0.07 is an efficient and environmentally friendly catalytic material.

We designed a dynamic continuous flow reactor to evaluate the practical application potential of CFOC0.07 (Fig. 10 (a)). The mixed solution of PMS and NOR flows into the reactor under the action of peristaltic pump to realize the degradation of NOR wastewater. The degradation efficiency of NOR in the CFOC0.07/PMS system is depicted in Fig. 10(b), showing that the efficiency can still reach 85.9 % after 300 min of continuous operation. This indicates that the reactor successfully achieves continuous and efficient degradation of NOR within the CFOC0.07/PMS system. Additionally, Fig. 10(c) presents the schematic diagram of the reactor. The CFOC0.07-Filter membrane offers several advantages, including high throughput, excellent stability and durability, an environmentally friendly preparation process, and convenient recycling and reuse. These features highlight its great potential for treating difficult-to-degrade antibiotic systems and practical wastewater treatment applications.

In this study, a carbon-loaded iron cobalt spinel high magnetic catalyst, CFOC0.07, was fabricated using a hydrothermal method and employed to catalyze the degradation of norfloxacin (NOR) by activated persulfate (PMS). Under optimal conditions, the CFOC0.07/PMS system removed 95.6 % of NOR (10 mg/L) with kobs of 0.1358 min−1. The enhanced catalytic capacity can be attributed to CQDs exposing more reactive active sites on the CoFe2O4 surface. The strong catalytic performance of the CFOC0.07/PMS system for NOR degradation is primarily due to the addition of CQDs, which effectively regulates the electron transfer efficiency of CFOC0.07. This acceleration of electron transfer increases the amount of reactive oxygen species and facilitates the regeneration of Co(II)/Co(III), thereby enhancing the redox capability. In combination with experiments such as Tafel polarization curve (Tafel) and impedance analyses, the results demonstrated that an increase in the electron transfer rate facilitates redox reactions in the CFOC0.07/PMS system and accelerates the reaction process of CFOC0.07 with PMS and NOR. Quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance analyses demonstrated that the catalytic oxidation mechanism in the CFOC0.07/PMS system involves both radical (•OH,
SO
4
∙ -
,
O
2
∙ -
) and non-radical (1O2) pathways, with
SO
4
∙ -
being the main active substance. The system exhibited excellent resistance to various coexisting anions in different complex water bodies. Additionally, the outstanding durability, stability, and recycling performance of CFOC0.07 have been substantiated through cycling experiments and a self-constructed continuous flow reactor. A combination of toxicity software analysis and seed toxicity experiments demonstrated that treatment of NOR wastewater with the CFOC0.07/PMS system resulted in a significant reduction in the biotoxicity of the wastewater. This study provides insights into the mechanism of degradation of organic pollutants by carbon materials loaded on highly magnetic Fe-Co based spinel catalysts and offers new ideas for water pollution control.
